miniLessons.title: Dairy Does More Than Add Liquid
miniLessons.lessonText:
Dairy ingredients can do different jobs depending on the recipe. Some dairy ingredients mainly add moisture, while others add richness, thickness, or a slightly tangy flavor.

Before choosing a substitute, it helps to ask what the dairy is doing in the recipe. For example, a thin dairy ingredient is easier to replace when the recipe only needs liquid. A thicker or tangier dairy ingredient needs a substitute that is closer in texture or flavor.

questions.prompt: A pancake recipe calls for 1 cup of buttermilk. Which substitution works best?
choices: 1 cup milk mixed with a small amount of lemon juice or vinegar
questions.feedback: Buttermilk adds moisture and acidity. Milk with a little lemon juice or vinegar is a closer match than water or butter.

miniLessons.title: Match Texture and Flavor
miniLessons.lessonText:
A good dairy substitute should be similar to the original ingredient. Texture matters because some dairy ingredients are thin and pourable, while others are thick and spoonable. Flavor matters too, especially when the dairy is used as a topping or dip.

For toppings, dips, and spreads, the substitute usually needs to stay thick enough to sit on the food instead of soaking into it. A substitute with a mild tangy flavor can also work better than something plain or watery.

questions.prompt: Which ingredient is the best easy substitute for sour cream in a baked potato topping?
questions.feedback: Plain Greek yogurt is thick and slightly tangy, so it works better as a sour cream substitute than thin liquids like milk or water.

miniLessons.title: Some Swaps Change the Final Dish
miniLessons.lessonText:
Even when a substitution works, it can still change the final result. A recipe may turn out thinner, less creamy, less rich, or slightly different in flavor.

This matters most in soups, sauces, dips, and desserts, where dairy often affects texture. When replacing a rich dairy ingredient, choose something that can replace both the liquid and some of the fat. Otherwise, the dish may still cook, but it may not feel as creamy.

questions.prompt: Which substitute works best for 1 cup of heavy cream in a soup when the cream does not need to be whipped?
questions.feedback: Heavy cream adds both liquid and fat. Milk with butter is a better match because it helps keep the soup creamy and rich.

miniLessons.title: Fresh and Dried Herbs Are Different
miniLessons.lessonText:
Fresh herbs and dried herbs can both add flavor, but they are not used in the same amount. Dried herbs usually have a stronger, more concentrated flavor because the water has been removed.

When swapping dried herbs for fresh herbs, you usually need less dried herb than fresh herb. A common rule is to use about one-third as much dried herb as fresh. This helps keep the flavor from becoming too strong.

questions.prompt: A recipe calls for 1 tablespoon of fresh basil, but you only have dried basil. What should you use?
questions.feedback: Dried herbs are stronger than fresh herbs, so you usually use less. Since 1 tablespoon equals 3 teaspoons, 1 teaspoon dried basil is about one-third of the fresh amount.

miniLessons.title: Think About the Herb’s Job
miniLessons.lessonText:
Herbs can be used for different reasons. Sometimes they add strong flavor while the food cooks. Other times, they are added at the end to make the dish look fresh, colorful, or finished.

When an herb is mostly being used as a garnish, the substitute should usually be fresh, mild, and green. A dried herb may add flavor, but it will not give the same fresh look or texture.

questions.prompt: Which herb is the best substitute for fresh parsley when it is mainly used as a garnish?
questions.feedback: Fresh cilantro is green and leafy, so it can work as a garnish. The other options do not provide the same fresh appearance or texture.

miniLessons.title: Some Seasonings Are Blends
miniLessons.lessonText:
Some seasonings are not single ingredients. They are blends made from several herbs or spices mixed together. If you are missing a seasoning blend, you may be able to replace it by combining spices that create a similar flavor.

When replacing a blend, think about the type of flavor it usually adds. Some blends are warm and sweet, some are savory and herbal, and some are smoky or spicy.

questions.prompt: Which mixture can replace 1 teaspoon of pumpkin pie spice?
choices: Salt, pepper, garlic powder, and onion powder
Chili powder, cumin, paprika, and cayenne
questions.feedback: Pumpkin pie spice is a warm baking spice blend. Cinnamon, ginger, nutmeg, and cloves create a similar warm flavor.

miniLessons.title: Binders Hold Ingredients Together
miniLessons.lessonText:
Some ingredients act like glue in a recipe. These are called binders. A binder helps ingredients stick together so the food does not crumble or fall apart.

Eggs are common binders in baked goods like muffins, cookies, and quick breads. If a recipe needs an egg mainly for binding, the substitute should also help hold the mixture together.

questions.prompt: Which mixture can replace one egg as a binder in muffins or cookies?
choices: 1 tablespoon ground flaxseed mixed with 3 tablespoons water
1 tablespoon salt mixed with 3 tablespoons sugar
questions.feedback: Ground flaxseed mixed with water creates a gel-like mixture that can help bind ingredients together in baked goods.

miniLessons.title: Thickeners Make Mixtures Less Runny
miniLessons.lessonText:
Thickeners help liquids become thicker. They are often used in sauces, gravies, soups, puddings, and fruit fillings.

Cornstarch and flour can both be used as thickeners, but they are not always used in the same amount. Cornstarch has stronger thickening power than flour, so you usually need more flour if you are using it as a substitute.

questions.prompt: A sauce recipe calls for 1 tablespoon of cornstarch, but you only have all-purpose flour. What should you use?
questions.feedback: Flour is a weaker thickener than cornstarch, so you usually need about twice as much flour to replace cornstarch.

miniLessons.title: Technique Matters With Thickeners
miniLessons.lessonText:
Using the right thickener is important, but how you add it matters too. Some starches can clump if they are added directly to hot liquid.

A slurry is a mixture of starch and cold liquid. Mixing the starch with cold water first helps it spread evenly before it goes into a hot sauce, soup, or filling.

questions.prompt: A recipe calls for a cornstarch slurry. What is the correct way to prepare the substitute before adding it to hot liquid?
choices: Mix the starch with cold water first
Sprinkle dry starch directly into boiling liquid
Mix the starch with oil only
Bake the starch before adding it
questions.feedback: A slurry is made by mixing starch with cold liquid first. This helps prevent clumps when it is added to hot liquid.

miniLessons.title: Doubling a Recipe
miniLessons.lessonText:
Scaling a recipe means changing the amount of food the recipe makes. If you want to make twice as much food, you usually double the ingredients.

Doubling means multiplying the original amount by 2. This works well for basic ingredients like broth, pasta, flour, rice, or vegetables.

questions.prompt: A soup recipe serves 4 people and uses 2 cups of broth. How much broth is needed to serve 8 people?
questions.feedback: The recipe is being doubled from 4 servings to 8 servings, so the broth also doubles from 2 cups to 4 cups.

miniLessons.title: Cutting a Recipe in Half
miniLessons.lessonText:
Sometimes you may want to make less food instead of more. If you want to make half as much food, you usually cut the ingredients in half.

Halving means dividing the original amount by 2. This is useful when a recipe makes too many servings or when you only have enough ingredients for a smaller batch.

questions.prompt: A cookie recipe makes 24 cookies and requires 3 cups of flour. How much flour is needed to make 12 cookies?
questions.feedback: The recipe is being cut in half from 24 cookies to 12 cookies, so the flour is also cut in half from 3 cups to 1 1/2 cups.

miniLessons.title: Keep the Same Ratio
miniLessons.lessonText:
Some recipes depend on ingredients staying in the same balance. This is called keeping the same ratio. If one ingredient increases, the other connected ingredients need to increase in the same way.

This matters in recipes like bread, sauces, dressings, and batters. If the ratio changes too much, the food may become too dry, too wet, too thick, or too thin.

questions.prompt: A bread recipe uses 4 cups of flour and 2 cups of water. If you use 6 cups of flour, how much water keeps the same ratio?
questions.feedback: The original recipe uses half as much water as flour. If the flour increases to 6 cups, the water should increase to 3 cups to keep the same ratio.

miniLessons.title: Start With the Most Likely Fix
miniLessons.lessonText:
When food tastes bland, it usually does not need a complicated fix right away. Often, it needs a small amount of seasoning. Salt is one of the most important flavor tools because it helps bring out the flavors that are already in the food.

The best first step is to add a little, stir, and taste again. Adding too much at once can make the food too salty, so small adjustments are safer.

questions.prompt: A pot of vegetable soup tastes bland even though the ingredients are fully cooked. What should you try first?
choices: Add a small amount of salt, stir, and taste again
Add a full cup of sugar
Throw the soup away and start over
Add uncooked pasta directly to the soup
questions.feedback: Bland food often needs a little more seasoning. Adding a small amount of salt and tasting again is safer than making a large change all at once.

miniLessons.title: Calm Down Spicy Heat
miniLessons.lessonText:
If a dish tastes too spicy, the goal is to reduce how strong the heat feels. Spicy flavors can often be softened by adding something creamy, fatty, or mild.

Ingredients like yogurt, sour cream, coconut milk, cream, or extra mild ingredients can help balance heat. This does not remove the spice completely, but it can make the dish easier to eat.

questions.prompt: A curry tastes too spicy for your guests. What is the best way to reduce the perceived heat?
choices: Add a creamy ingredient like yogurt or coconut milk
Add a large amount of black pepper
Boil it until the spice disappears
questions.feedback: Creamy or fatty ingredients can help soften the feeling of spicy heat. Adding more spicy ingredients would make the problem worse.

miniLessons.title: Fix Saltiness by Spreading It Out
miniLessons.lessonText:
If a dish is too salty, adding sugar usually does not solve the problem. The most reliable fix is to spread the salt out by adding more unsalted ingredients or more liquid.

For soups and stews, this might mean adding unsalted broth, water, vegetables, beans, rice, or potatoes. The goal is to make a larger amount of food so the salty flavor is less concentrated.

questions.prompt: A stew tastes too salty. Which emergency adjustment is most effective?
choices: Add more unsalted ingredients or liquid
questions.feedback: Saltiness is best reduced by diluting or stretching the dish with unsalted ingredients or liquid. This spreads the salt through more food.

miniLessons.title: Thickening Food That Is Too Thin
miniLessons.lessonText:
Sometimes soups, sauces, or gravies turn out thinner than expected. The goal is to thicken the food without making it lumpy.

One safe way to thicken a hot liquid is to make a slurry. A slurry is made by mixing a thickener, like cornstarch or flour, with a small amount of cold liquid first. Once it is smooth, it can be stirred into the hot food.

questions.prompt: A soup is much thinner than intended. What is the best way to thicken it quickly without creating lumps?
choices: Mix a thickener with cold liquid first, then stir it into the soup
Dump dry flour directly into the hot soup
Add ice cubes to the soup
Turn off the heat and add sugar
questions.feedback: Mixing the thickener with cold liquid first helps prevent lumps. This makes it easier to stir smoothly into the soup.

miniLessons.title: Thinning Food That Is Too Thick
miniLessons.lessonText:
Sometimes a sauce, soup, or batter becomes thicker than you want. The best fix is to add a small amount of liquid at a time.

Adding liquid slowly gives you more control. If you add too much at once, the food can quickly become too thin. Stir after each small addition and check the texture before adding more.

questions.prompt: A cooked sauce is too thick. What is the best way to thin it while maintaining control of the texture?
choices: Add a small amount of liquid at a time and stir
Add a full cup of liquid immediately
questions.feedback: Adding liquid slowly helps you control the texture. A large amount of liquid could make the sauce too thin.

miniLessons.title: Fixing Watery Foods
miniLessons.lessonText:
Some foods become watery when they have too much liquid or not enough structure. This can happen with foods like mashed potatoes, rice, sauces, or casseroles.

To fix watery food, you usually need to either absorb the extra moisture or let some of it cook off. The best choice depends on the food. For mashed potatoes, adding more potato flakes or cooked potato can help absorb extra moisture and make the texture thicker.

questions.prompt: Mashed potatoes have become too watery. Which adjustment is most likely to improve their texture?
choices: Add more cooked potato or potato flakes
questions.feedback: Cooked potato or potato flakes can absorb extra moisture and help thicken watery mashed potatoes.

miniLessons.title: Sautéing vs. Pan-Searing
miniLessons.lessonText:
Sautéing and pan-searing both use a pan, heat, and a small amount of oil, but they are used for different results.

Sautéing usually means cooking smaller pieces of food while stirring or moving them around in the pan. It is often used for vegetables, garlic, onions, or small pieces of meat.

Pan-searing usually means cooking food over higher heat to create a browned crust on the outside. It is often used for larger pieces of meat, fish, tofu, or vegetables.

questions.prompt: What is the main difference between sautéing and pan-searing?
choices: Sautéing usually cooks smaller pieces while moving them, while pan-searing creates a browned crust
Sautéing only uses water, while pan-searing only uses sugar
Sautéing is always done in the oven, while pan-searing is always done in the microwave
Sautéing means freezing food before cooking it
questions.feedback: Sautéing usually involves smaller pieces that are moved around in the pan. Pan-searing focuses on browning the outside of the food.

miniLessons.title: Do Not Overcrowd the Pan
miniLessons.lessonText:
Food needs space in the pan to cook well. When too much food is added at once, the pan temperature can drop and moisture gets trapped.

Instead of browning, overcrowded food may steam. This can make vegetables softer and meat less browned. Cooking in smaller batches can help food brown better and cook more evenly.

questions.prompt: What usually happens when too much food is placed in a sauté pan at once?
choices: The food may steam instead of brown
questions.feedback: Too much food can trap moisture and lower the pan temperature. This often causes food to steam instead of brown.

miniLessons.title: Dry Food Browns Better
miniLessons.lessonText:
Moisture makes browning harder. If meat is wet on the surface, the pan has to evaporate that moisture before browning can really begin.

Patting meat dry with a paper towel helps the surface brown more easily. This is especially useful when pan-searing chicken, steak, pork, fish, or tofu.

questions.prompt: Why should meat usually be patted dry before pan-searing?
choices: To help the surface brown better
To remove all flavor from the meat
To make the pan stop heating
questions.feedback: A dry surface browns better because the pan can focus on searing instead of evaporating extra moisture.

miniLessons.title: Recognizing a Full Boil
miniLessons.lessonText:
Boiling is one of the strongest forms of cooking with liquid. A full boil has large bubbles that rise quickly and constantly break the surface.

Boiling is useful for foods that can handle stronger movement, like pasta, potatoes, or some vegetables. Since the water is moving a lot, boiling is not always the best choice for delicate foods.

questions.prompt: Which description best matches a full boil?
choices: Large bubbles constantly rising and breaking the surface
A few tiny bubbles sitting at the bottom of the pot
questions.feedback: A full boil has strong, active bubbles that keep rising and breaking the surface of the liquid.

miniLessons.title: Simmering Is Gentler Than Boiling
miniLessons.lessonText:
Simmering uses lower heat than boiling. Instead of large, rapid bubbles, a simmer has smaller bubbles that rise more gently.

Simmering is useful when food needs time to cook without being tossed around too much. Soups, stews, sauces, and beans are often simmered because gentle cooking helps flavors develop while keeping the texture under control.

questions.prompt: How does simmering differ from boiling?
choices: Simmering uses gentler heat with smaller bubbles
Simmering means cooking with no heat
Simmering only happens in the freezer
Simmering uses larger bubbles than boiling
questions.feedback: Simmering is gentler than boiling. It uses lower heat and smaller bubbles.

miniLessons.title: Poaching Uses Very Gentle Heat
miniLessons.lessonText:
Poaching is even gentler than simmering. The liquid is hot, but it should not bubble strongly. This makes poaching useful for delicate foods that could break apart, dry out, or become tough with stronger heat.

Foods like eggs, fish, or tender chicken can be poached because the gentle liquid cooks them slowly and evenly.

questions.prompt: Which cooking method uses liquid kept below a simmer to cook delicate foods gently?
questions.feedback: Poaching uses hot liquid below a simmer. It is gentle enough for delicate foods like eggs or fish.

miniLessons.title: Roasting and Baking Use the Oven Differently
miniLessons.lessonText:
Roasting and baking both use dry heat in the oven, but they are usually used for different types of food.

Roasting is often used for foods that already have structure, like vegetables, potatoes, chicken, or meat. The goal is often to brown the outside and develop deeper flavor.

Baking is often used for foods that need to set or rise, like cookies, cakes, muffins, and bread. These foods usually depend more on careful measuring and timing.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains the traditional difference between roasting and baking?
choices: Roasting is often used for structured foods like vegetables or meats, while baking is often used for foods like breads, cakes, or cookies
Roasting only uses cold air, while baking only uses water
Roasting is done in the microwave, while baking is done in the freezer
Roasting and baking always mean exactly the same thing
questions.feedback: Roasting and baking both use oven heat, but roasting is often used for foods like vegetables or meats, while baking is often used for foods that set or rise.

miniLessons.title: Roasting Needs Space
miniLessons.lessonText:
When roasting vegetables, space matters. If vegetables are piled on top of each other, they trap steam and may turn soft instead of browned.

Spreading vegetables in a single layer helps hot air reach more of the surface. This makes browning and crisp edges more likely.

questions.prompt: Why are vegetables often spread in a single layer before roasting?
choices: To help them brown instead of steam
To make them cook only on one side
questions.feedback: A single layer gives vegetables more space and helps moisture escape. This makes browning easier.

miniLessons.title: Baking Needs Accuracy
miniLessons.lessonText:
Baking is often less forgiving than other types of cooking. Ingredients like flour, sugar, eggs, baking powder, baking soda, and liquid work together to create the final texture.

If the measurements are too far off, baked goods may not rise, set, spread, or hold together correctly. Careful measuring helps the recipe turn out closer to the way it was designed.

questions.prompt: Why is accurate measuring especially important when baking?
choices: Baking depends on ingredient balance for texture and structure
Measuring makes food cook without heat
Baking recipes do not use ingredients
Measuring only changes the color of the oven
questions.feedback: Baking depends on the balance between ingredients. If that balance changes too much, the texture and structure can change.

miniLessons.title: Protect Your Fingers
miniLessons.lessonText:
The safest way to hold food while slicing is to keep your fingertips tucked back. This is often called a claw grip.

With a claw grip, your fingertips curl under slightly while your knuckles face the knife. The side of the knife can rest lightly against your knuckles as a guide, while your fingertips stay out of the blade’s path.

questions.prompt: What is the safest way to hold food while slicing it with a chef's knife?
choices: Curl your fingertips back in a claw grip
Keep your fingers flat and stretched out
Hold the food from the side closest to the blade
Use your palm to press down on the blade
questions.feedback: A claw grip keeps your fingertips tucked back and helps protect them while slicing.

miniLessons.title: Keep the Cutting Board Stable
miniLessons.lessonText:
A cutting board should not slide around while you are using a knife. If the board moves, the knife can slip and become harder to control.

To keep the board steady, place a damp towel or non-slip mat underneath it. This creates grip between the board and the counter.

questions.prompt: What should you place under a cutting board to keep it from sliding?
choices: A damp towel or non-slip mat
questions.feedback: A damp towel or non-slip mat helps hold the cutting board in place so it does not slide while cutting.

miniLessons.title: Sharp Knives Are Safer Than Dull Knives
miniLessons.lessonText:
A sharp knife may sound more dangerous, but it is usually safer than a dull knife. A sharp knife cuts more easily, so you do not have to push as hard.

A dull knife can slip because it struggles to cut through food. When you press harder, it becomes easier to lose control. Keeping knives sharp helps make cutting smoother and safer.

questions.prompt: Why is a sharp knife usually safer than a dull knife?
choices: It cuts more easily, so less force is needed
It bends when it touches food
questions.feedback: A sharp knife cuts with less force. A dull knife can slip more easily because you have to push harder.

miniLessons.title: Use Heavy Pans for Browning
miniLessons.lessonText:
Some foods need strong, steady heat to brown well. This is especially true when searing foods like steak, chicken, tofu, or vegetables.

A heavy pan, such as cast iron or stainless steel, is useful because it holds heat well. When the food touches the hot surface, it can develop a browned crust instead of just steaming.

questions.prompt: Which pan is best suited for searing a steak and developing a browned crust?
questions.feedback: A cast iron skillet holds heat well, which helps steak develop a browned crust.

miniLessons.title: Use Nonstick for Delicate Foods
miniLessons.lessonText:
Some foods are delicate and can tear or stick easily while cooking. Eggs are a common example because they cook quickly and can break apart when they stick to the pan.

A nonstick pan helps delicate foods release more easily. This makes it useful for scrambled eggs, omelets, pancakes, and other foods that need gentle handling.

questions.prompt: Which pan is generally best for cooking delicate eggs with minimal sticking?
questions.feedback: A nonstick skillet helps delicate eggs release from the pan with less sticking.

miniLessons.title: Use a Sheet Pan for Roasting
miniLessons.lessonText:
Roasting works best when food has enough space for hot air to move around it. A wide, shallow surface helps food brown instead of steam.

A baking sheet or sheet pan is a good choice for roasting vegetables because it spreads the food out in the oven. If the vegetables are piled too closely together, they may become soft instead of browned.

questions.prompt: Which baking surface is most appropriate for roasting vegetables until browned?
choices: A sheet pan or baking sheet
questions.feedback: A sheet pan gives vegetables space so they can roast and brown instead of steaming in a crowded dish.

miniLessons.title: Match the Shape and Job
miniLessons.lessonText:
You do not always need the exact kitchen tool a recipe mentions. Sometimes another clean, safe object can do the same job.

If you need to roll out dough, the tool needs to be smooth, sturdy, and easy to roll. A clean bottle or sturdy cup can work because it has a similar shape to a rolling pin.

questions.prompt: You need to roll out dough but do not own a rolling pin. What is the best substitute?
choices: A clean bottle or sturdy cup
questions.feedback: A clean bottle or sturdy cup can roll over dough in a similar way to a rolling pin.

miniLessons.title: Match the Action
miniLessons.lessonText:
When replacing a kitchen tool, think about the action the tool performs. A potato masher presses soft food down and breaks it apart.

If you do not have a potato masher, a sturdy fork can do a similar job. It may take longer, but it can still press and break up cooked potatoes.

questions.prompt: You need to mash potatoes but do not have a potato masher. What should you use?
questions.feedback: A sturdy fork can press and break apart soft potatoes, making it a useful substitute for a potato masher.

miniLessons.title: Make Safe Setup Swaps
miniLessons.lessonText:
Some kitchen tools are really setups, not just single items. A double boiler is used to heat delicate foods gently, like chocolate, without placing them directly over strong heat.

A makeshift double boiler can be made by placing a heat-safe bowl over a pot of gently simmering water. The bowl should sit above the water, not directly in it. This lets steam warm the bowl gently.

questions.prompt: You need a makeshift double boiler to melt chocolate gently. Which setup is best?
choices: A heat-safe bowl set over a pot of gently simmering water
A plastic bowl directly on a burner
Chocolate placed directly in a hot dry pan on high heat
A paper plate over boiling water
questions.feedback: A heat-safe bowl over gently simmering water creates gentle heat, which helps melt chocolate without burning it.

miniLessons.title: Dairy Does More Than Add Liquid
miniLessons.lessonText:
Dairy swaps work best when you think about the job of the ingredient first. In baked goods, dairy might add moisture, tang, fat, or tenderness. A substitute should replace the most important job, not just look similar.

For medium questions, pay attention to clues like “moisture and tang” or “richness.” Yogurt and sour cream are both thick and tangy, while whole milk has more fat than skim milk. If a substitute is missing fat, a small amount of butter can sometimes help.

questions.prompt: A muffin batter uses plain yogurt to add moisture and tang. Which substitute is closest?
questions.feedback: Sour cream is thick and tangy, so it can replace plain yogurt better than a thin or dry ingredient.

questions.prompt: You need whole milk for quick bread, but you only have skim milk. Which change best restores some richness?
choices: Use skim milk with a small amount of melted butter
questions.feedback: Skim milk has less fat than whole milk. Adding a little melted butter helps replace some richness.

miniLessons.title: Match Texture and Flavor
miniLessons.lessonText:
Some dairy ingredients matter because of their texture. Dips, spreads, casseroles, and fillings often need dairy that is thick, soft, or spoonable instead of thin and pourable.

For medium questions, compare the way the ingredient behaves. Cream cheese needs another smooth, spreadable cheese. Ricotta needs something soft and mild that can work in a baked dish. Thin liquids usually will not replace thick dairy very well.

questions.prompt: A cheesecake-style dip needs cream cheese. Which option will give the closest creamy texture?
questions.feedback: Neufchâtel cheese has a texture similar to cream cheese, so it works well in creamy dips.

questions.prompt: A baked pasta dish calls for ricotta, but you do not have any. Which substitute is most reasonable?
questions.feedback: Cottage cheese is soft and mild, making it a reasonable substitute for ricotta in many casseroles.

miniLessons.title: Some Swaps Change the Final Dish
miniLessons.lessonText:
Rich dairy ingredients can affect both texture and flavor. Half-and-half, cream, evaporated milk, and whole milk are not identical because they have different amounts of fat, water, and concentration.

For medium questions, look for whether the substitute needs to be diluted, enriched, or combined with another ingredient. Evaporated milk is concentrated, while half-and-half is between milk and cream in richness.

questions.prompt: A pasta sauce calls for half-and-half. Which substitute best matches its balance of milkiness and richness?
choices: Equal parts milk and heavy cream
questions.feedback: Half-and-half is made from milk and cream, so combining milk and cream gives a similar richness.

questions.prompt: A soup recipe uses whole milk, but you only have evaporated milk. What should you do before using it?
choices: Use it as a dry powder
questions.feedback: Evaporated milk is concentrated, so diluting it with water makes it closer to regular milk.

miniLessons.title: Fresh and Dried Herbs Are Different
miniLessons.lessonText:
Fresh herbs, dried herbs, and powdered seasonings can all add flavor, but they are not used in the same amounts. Dried herbs and powders are usually more concentrated because much of the water has been removed.

For medium questions, watch for measurement clues. A common starting point is about one-third as much dried herb as fresh herb. Garlic powder is also much stronger by volume than fresh garlic, so only a small amount is needed.

questions.prompt: A soup calls for 2 tablespoons of fresh parsley, but you only have dried parsley. What is a reasonable starting amount?
questions.feedback: Dried herbs are stronger than fresh herbs, so about one-third as much dried herb is a good starting point.

questions.prompt: A recipe uses one fresh garlic clove, but you only have garlic powder. Which amount is most appropriate?
questions.feedback: Garlic powder is concentrated, so a small amount can replace one fresh garlic clove.

miniLessons.title: Think About the Herb’s Job
miniLessons.lessonText:
Herb substitutes should match the flavor role of the original herb. Some herbs are earthy and savory, some are bright and fresh, and some are better for roasted foods than delicate garnishes.

For medium questions, use the food as a clue. Thyme, marjoram, and rosemary are savory herbs often used in cooked dishes. Warm baking spices or sweet ingredients are usually not good replacements for savory herbs.

questions.prompt: A savory recipe calls for thyme, but none is available. Which herb is the most suitable substitute?
questions.feedback: Marjoram has a mild herbal flavor that can work in many savory recipes that use thyme.

questions.prompt: You are roasting chicken and are out of rosemary. Which herb would be the best substitute?
questions.feedback: Thyme is savory and herbal, so it can work well in many roasted chicken recipes.

miniLessons.title: Some Seasonings Are Blends
miniLessons.lessonText:
Seasoning blends are made from smaller ingredients. If you know the flavor family of the blend, you can often make a close substitute with individual herbs or spices.

For medium questions, separate warm baking blends from savory herb blends. Allspice has a warm flavor similar to cinnamon, nutmeg, and cloves. Italian seasoning usually points toward dried herbs like basil, oregano, thyme, and rosemary.

questions.prompt: A spice mix needs the warm flavor of allspice. Which blend is the best substitute?
questions.feedback: Allspice has warm baking-spice flavors, so cinnamon, nutmeg, and cloves can create a similar effect.

questions.prompt: You are out of Italian seasoning. Which homemade mix is the closest match?
choices: Dried basil, oregano, thyme, and rosemary
questions.feedback: Italian seasoning is usually an herbal blend, so basil, oregano, thyme, and rosemary are a close match.

miniLessons.title: Binders Hold Ingredients Together
miniLessons.lessonText:
Binders help a mixture stay together instead of crumbling apart. Eggs are common binders, but fruit purees and seed mixtures can also help in some baked goods.

For medium questions, look at the type of recipe. Applesauce can work well in brownies because it adds moisture and binding. Mashed banana is especially reasonable in banana bread because its flavor already fits the recipe.

questions.prompt: A brownie recipe needs one egg mainly to help hold the batter together. Which fruit-based substitute makes sense?
questions.feedback: Applesauce can help bind and add moisture when the egg is mainly used for binding.

questions.prompt: A banana bread recipe is missing one egg used for binding. Which substitute fits the recipe best?
questions.feedback: Mashed banana can help bind ingredients and adds moisture, especially in banana bread.

miniLessons.title: Thickeners Make Mixtures Less Runny
miniLessons.lessonText:
Thickeners help liquids become thicker, but different starches work better in different situations. Cornstarch, arrowroot, and tapioca starch can all thicken, but the best choice depends on the food.

For medium questions, notice whether the recipe is a fruit filling, pudding, sauce, or gravy. Arrowroot is often a good cornstarch substitute in fruit fillings, while tapioca starch can help thicken soft mixtures like pudding.

questions.prompt: A fruit filling needs cornstarch, but you only have arrowroot starch. What should you do?
choices: Use four times as much sugar
Use an equal amount of arrowroot starch
questions.feedback: Arrowroot starch can often replace cornstarch in equal amounts, especially in fruit fillings.

questions.prompt: A pudding needs a starch thickener, but cornstarch is unavailable. Which substitute is most suitable?
questions.feedback: Tapioca starch can help thicken puddings and other soft mixtures when cornstarch is not available.

miniLessons.title: Technique Matters With Thickeners
miniLessons.lessonText:
The right substitute can still fail if the texture is handled poorly. Lumps need smoothing or straining, while savory binders need something that can absorb moisture and hold ingredients together.

For medium questions, think about the problem you are fixing. Lumpy gravy needs whisking or straining. A savory loaf that is missing bread crumbs needs another dry binder, such as oats or crushed crackers.

questions.prompt: Your gravy has small lumps after thickening. What is the best way to improve it?
choices: Add more dry flour without mixing
Whisk it well and strain it if needed
Add whole eggs directly to the hot gravy
questions.feedback: Whisking can break up small lumps, and straining can remove lumps that do not smooth out.

questions.prompt: A savory baked loaf needs bread crumbs for binding, but you are out. Which substitute is reasonable?
questions.feedback: Rolled oats or crushed crackers can absorb moisture and help hold a savory loaf together.

miniLessons.title: Doubling a Recipe
miniLessons.lessonText:
Scaling up means finding how much larger the new batch is and multiplying the ingredients by that amount. When the number of servings doubles, the basic ingredient amounts usually double too.

For medium questions, compare the original amount to the new amount. If pancakes go from 10 to 20, that is double. If servings go from 6 to 12, that is also double, even when the ingredient amount is a fraction.

questions.prompt: A pancake recipe makes 10 pancakes with 2 eggs. How many eggs are needed for 20 pancakes?
questions.feedback: The recipe is doubled from 10 pancakes to 20 pancakes, so the eggs double from 2 to 4.

questions.prompt: A recipe serves 6 and uses 1 1/2 teaspoons of salt. How much salt is needed to serve 12?
questions.feedback: The recipe is doubled from 6 servings to 12 servings, so the salt doubles to 3 teaspoons.

miniLessons.title: Cutting a Recipe in Half
miniLessons.lessonText:
Scaling down uses the same idea as scaling up, but the multiplier is less than 1. If the new batch is half the size, divide the ingredient amounts in half.

For medium questions, look for recipes being reduced from 8 servings to 4, or from 16 pieces to 8. Fractions can be divided too: half of 3/4 cup is 3/8 cup.

questions.prompt: A pasta recipe serves 8 and uses 2 pounds of pasta. How much pasta is needed for 4 servings?
questions.feedback: Four servings is half of eight servings, so the pasta is cut in half from 2 pounds to 1 pound.

questions.prompt: A brownie recipe makes 16 squares and uses 3/4 cup cocoa powder. How much cocoa is needed for 8 squares?
questions.feedback: Eight squares is half the recipe, so 3/4 cup is cut in half to 3/8 cup.

miniLessons.title: Keep the Same Ratio
miniLessons.lessonText:
Some recipes depend on ingredients staying in the same balance. Dressings, sauces, bread, and batters can change texture or flavor if the ratio shifts too much.

For medium questions, identify the relationship between ingredients or batch size. A 3-to-1 dressing ratio means the larger ingredient stays three times the smaller one. A sauce reduced to one-third needs one-third of each ingredient.

questions.prompt: A salad dressing uses 3 tablespoons oil for every 1 tablespoon vinegar. How much oil is needed for 4 tablespoons vinegar?
questions.feedback: The ratio is 3 parts oil to 1 part vinegar. Four tablespoons of vinegar need 12 tablespoons of oil.

questions.prompt: A sauce recipe makes 3 cups and uses 3/4 cup tomato paste. How much tomato paste is needed for 1 cup of sauce?
questions.feedback: One cup of sauce is one-third of the recipe, so 3/4 cup divided by 3 equals 1/4 cup.

miniLessons.title: Start With the Most Likely Fix
miniLessons.lessonText:
Flat or bland food does not always need more salt. If a dish is already salty enough but still tastes dull, it may need brightness, freshness, or acidity.

For medium questions, pay attention to phrases like “already salty enough.” That clue means more salt is probably the wrong move. A small squeeze of citrus, vinegar, or fresh herbs can make flavors feel more awake without increasing saltiness.

questions.prompt: A bean chili tastes flat, but it already has enough salt. What could add needed brightness?
questions.feedback: If the dish is salty enough but still tastes flat, a small amount of acid can brighten the flavor.

questions.prompt: A finished rice dish tastes one-dimensional but is already salty. Which addition is most likely to help?
choices: Fresh herbs or a little lemon
questions.feedback: Fresh herbs or a little acid can add brightness without making the rice saltier.

miniLessons.title: Calm Down Spicy Heat
miniLessons.lessonText:
When food is too spicy, the goal is to make the heat feel less intense without ruining the dish. Creamy, fatty, or mild ingredients can soften spicy heat.

For medium questions, notice whether the dish should stay thick. Yogurt, sour cream, coconut milk, or cream can reduce perceived heat better than water because they add richness instead of simply thinning the food.

questions.prompt: A pot of chili is too spicy, but you do not want to make it watery. What is the best adjustment?
choices: Stir in sour cream or plain yogurt
questions.feedback: Creamy ingredients can soften perceived heat without thinning the chili as much as water would.

questions.prompt: A spicy soup is too intense for your roommate. Which add-in would most likely make it taste milder?
questions.feedback: Creamy or fatty ingredients can make spicy heat feel less harsh.

miniLessons.title: Fix Saltiness by Spreading It Out
miniLessons.lessonText:
Saltiness is a concentration problem. The most reliable fix is to spread the salt through more unsalted food or liquid so each bite tastes less salty.

For medium questions, choose an unsalted version of something that already belongs in the dish. Soup can be stretched with unsalted broth and vegetables. Salty rice can be mixed with plain cooked rice instead of adding sugar or more seasoning.

questions.prompt: A soup tastes too salty and there is room in the pot. Which fix is most reliable?
choices: Add unsalted broth and extra vegetables
questions.feedback: Adding unsalted liquid and ingredients spreads the salt through more food.

questions.prompt: A rice dish is too salty, but the texture is good. What is the best way to make it easier to eat?
choices: Cook it until dry and crunchy
questions.feedback: Unsalted rice increases the total amount of food and lowers the salty concentration.

miniLessons.title: Thickening Food That Is Too Thin
miniLessons.lessonText:
Thin sauces, soups, and fillings often need a thickener, but adding dry starch straight into hot liquid can create lumps. A slurry helps the thickener spread evenly.

For medium questions, look for words like “smooth,” “without clumps,” or “runny.” Mixing starch with cold liquid first is usually safer. If lumps have already formed, whisking and straining can improve the texture.

questions.prompt: A pan sauce looks watery. What is the best way to thicken it without clumps?
choices: Turn off the heat and add sugar
Stir in a smooth slurry made with cold liquid
questions.feedback: A slurry spreads the thickener evenly before it goes into the hot sauce.

questions.prompt: A fruit filling is runny while cooking. Which method gives the smoothest thickening?
choices: Mix starch with cold water first, then stir it in
Sprinkle dry starch over the top and leave it
questions.feedback: Mixing starch with cold liquid first helps prevent lumps.

miniLessons.title: Thinning Food That Is Too Thick
miniLessons.lessonText:
Thick sauces can usually be loosened by adding liquid slowly. Separated or grainy sauces need gentler handling because harsh heat can make the texture worse.

For medium questions, decide whether the food is simply too thick or starting to break. Too thick means add a little liquid and stir. Grainy or separated often means lower the heat, whisk gently, and add a small splash of warm liquid.

questions.prompt: A cheese sauce becomes too thick after sitting for a few minutes. What should you do?
choices: Whisk in a small splash of warm milk
questions.feedback: Adding warm liquid slowly helps loosen the sauce while keeping control of the texture.

questions.prompt: A gravy is thicker than you wanted right before serving. What is the safest fix?
choices: Add a full cup of broth all at once
Whisk in small splashes of broth
questions.feedback: Small additions of liquid help thin the gravy without overshooting.

miniLessons.title: Fixing Watery Foods
miniLessons.lessonText:
Texture problems can come from too much moisture or not enough moisture. Wet foods may need extra moisture cooked off, while dry mixtures may need a small amount of liquid or fat.

For medium questions, match the fix to the direction of the problem. Wet rice can often be uncovered so steam escapes. Dry, crumbly cookie dough needs a controlled addition of moisture or softened fat, not more dry flour.

questions.prompt: A batch of cooked rice is slightly too wet. What is the best immediate fix?
choices: Uncover it and let extra moisture steam off over low heat
questions.feedback: Letting extra moisture steam off can help wet rice become less soggy.

questions.prompt: Cookie dough is dry and crumbly and will not hold together. What is the best adjustment?
choices: Add a small amount of liquid or softened fat
Bake it immediately without changing anything
questions.feedback: Dry, crumbly dough usually needs a small amount of moisture or fat to help it come together.

miniLessons.title: Sautéing vs. Pan-Searing
miniLessons.lessonText:
Sautéing and pan-searing both use a pan, heat, and some fat, but the movement and goal are different. Sautéing usually uses smaller pieces that move often. Pan-searing usually keeps larger pieces in place long enough to brown.

For medium questions, use the food size and movement as clues. Small diced onions stirred often point to sautéing. Larger tofu slabs or pieces of meat left mostly still are more likely being pan-seared.

questions.prompt: You are cooking small diced onions while stirring them often in a little oil. Which method are you most likely using?
questions.feedback: Sautéing usually cooks smaller pieces of food while they are moved around in a pan.

questions.prompt: You want browned tofu slabs with a crust on the outside and little movement in the pan. Which method fits best?
questions.feedback: Pan-searing focuses on browning the outside of food against a hot pan.

miniLessons.title: Do Not Overcrowd the Pan
miniLessons.lessonText:
Browning needs heat, space, and moisture control. If a pan is crowded, food releases steam and has a harder time browning. If pieces are different sizes, they may cook unevenly.

For medium questions, look for clues about liquid collecting in the pan or uneven cooking. Smaller batches help crowded food brown. Similar-sized pieces help vegetables finish cooking at about the same time.

questions.prompt: Vegetables in a crowded skillet are releasing liquid and not browning. What should you do next time?
choices: Cover the pan tightly from the start
questions.feedback: Cooking in smaller batches gives moisture room to escape and helps browning happen.

questions.prompt: Why are vegetables often cut into similar-sized pieces before sautéing?
choices: So they do not need heat
So they all taste like sugar
questions.feedback: Similar-sized pieces cook at similar rates, which helps avoid undercooked or overcooked pieces.

miniLessons.title: Dry Food Browns Better
miniLessons.lessonText:
Surface moisture slows browning because the pan must evaporate water before a crust can form. This matters for meat, tofu, and foods that have been sitting in marinade.

For medium questions, notice whether the food is wet from packaging or marinade. Patting or blotting the surface dry helps the food sear instead of steam, especially when you want a browned crust.

questions.prompt: Chicken breasts are wet from the package before searing. What should you do first?
choices: Add extra water to the pan
Pat the surface dry with a paper towel
questions.feedback: Drying the surface helps the chicken brown instead of steaming.

questions.prompt: A tofu slice has extra marinade on the outside before searing. Why might blotting it help?
choices: It removes the need for heat
Less surface moisture makes browning easier
questions.feedback: Extra moisture has to evaporate before browning can happen, so blotting helps searing.

miniLessons.title: Recognizing a Full Boil
miniLessons.lessonText:
Boiling uses strong, active bubbles. Pasta usually needs water that is fully boiling, plus enough movement and stirring to keep pieces from sticking.

For medium questions, check what the bubbles are doing. A few bubbles around the edge is not a full boil yet. After pasta is added, stirring helps separate the pieces while the water returns to a boil.

questions.prompt: Your pasta water has only a few small bubbles around the edge. What should you do before adding pasta?
choices: Add pasta immediately and turn off the heat
questions.feedback: Pasta usually cooks best when added to water that has reached a full boil.

questions.prompt: After pasta is added, the boiling slows down and pieces start sticking. What should you do?
choices: Stir and let the water return to a boil
questions.feedback: Stirring helps prevent sticking while the water returns to a boil.

miniLessons.title: Simmering Is Gentler Than Boiling
miniLessons.lessonText:
Simmering is controlled, gentle bubbling. It is useful when food needs time to cook, soften, or develop flavor without being tossed around by aggressive boiling.

For medium questions, think about control. Soups and stews often simmer because hard boiling can make textures rough. If a pot boils over, lowering the heat or moving it off the burner is the first safe response.

questions.prompt: Why are soups and stews often simmered instead of boiled hard?
choices: Gentler bubbles help texture and flavor develop
Simmering only works in the freezer
questions.feedback: Simmering cooks steadily without tossing ingredients around as much as a hard boil.

questions.prompt: A pot starts boiling over. What should you do first?
choices: Cover it tighter and walk away
Lower the heat or carefully move it off the burner
questions.feedback: Reducing the heat source helps stop the boil-over safely.

miniLessons.title: Poaching Uses Very Gentle Heat
miniLessons.lessonText:
Poaching uses hot liquid below a strong simmer. It is gentle enough for delicate foods that could break apart or become tough in rapidly boiling water.

For medium questions, look for foods like eggs, fish, or tender chicken. If the liquid boils too hard, delicate foods can fall apart, toughen, or cook unevenly.

questions.prompt: Which food is commonly cooked with gentle poaching?
questions.feedback: Eggs and fish are delicate enough that gentle poaching can cook them without rough bubbling.

questions.prompt: Why should poaching liquid stay below a vigorous boil?
choices: Strong boiling can break delicate foods apart or make them tough
Boiling turns the liquid into ice
Boiling removes the need for cooking
questions.feedback: Poaching should stay gentle so delicate foods keep their shape and texture.

miniLessons.title: Roasting and Baking Use the Oven Differently
miniLessons.lessonText:
Roasting and baking both use dry oven heat, but they often aim for different results. Roasting usually focuses on browning structured foods, while baking depends on a recipe setting, rising, or spreading correctly.

For medium questions, use the food goal as the clue. Potato pieces that should get browned and crisp point to roasting. Cookies need the oven preheated so they start baking at the intended temperature.

questions.prompt: Which oven method is best for seasoned potato pieces that should become browned and crisp?
questions.feedback: Roasting uses dry oven heat and is a good method for browning potatoes.

questions.prompt: Why should an oven usually be preheated before baking cookies?
choices: So the cookies begin baking at the intended temperature
questions.feedback: Preheating helps cookies bake at the temperature the recipe was designed for.

miniLessons.title: Roasting Needs Space
miniLessons.lessonText:
Roasting works best when hot air can reach the food and surface moisture can escape. Oil and occasional turning can help more surfaces brown.

For medium questions, watch for clues about browning. A moderate amount of oil helps roasted vegetables brown and avoid drying out. Stirring or turning halfway through helps expose different sides to oven heat.

questions.prompt: Why should roasted vegetables usually be tossed with a moderate amount of oil?
choices: Oil helps browning and prevents too much drying
questions.feedback: A moderate amount of oil helps vegetables brown and develop better texture.

questions.prompt: Why are roasted vegetables sometimes stirred halfway through cooking?
choices: To help them brown more evenly
To stop the oven from heating
questions.feedback: Turning or stirring exposes more sides to heat, which helps vegetables brown more evenly.

miniLessons.title: Baking Needs Accuracy
miniLessons.lessonText:
Baking depends on timing, structure, and steady oven heat. Cakes, cookies, muffins, and breads can change if they lose heat or come out before they finish setting.

For medium questions, look for clues about the center, rise, and oven door. A cake removed too early may sink or stay undercooked. Opening the oven repeatedly can lower the temperature and affect structure.

questions.prompt: What can happen if a cake is removed before the center has set?
choices: The center may sink or stay undercooked
The cake turns into soup instantly
The cake becomes raw flour again
questions.feedback: If the center has not set, the cake may collapse, sink, or remain undercooked.

questions.prompt: Why should an oven door not be opened repeatedly while a cake is baking?
choices: Opening the door makes the cake cook without heat
Heat loss can affect how the cake rises and sets
questions.feedback: Repeatedly opening the oven door can lower the temperature and affect the cake’s structure.

miniLessons.title: Protect Your Fingers
miniLessons.lessonText:
Knife safety starts before the cut. Food should be stable, and your guiding hand should protect your fingertips while helping control the cut.

For medium questions, pay attention to shape and hand position. Round foods like potatoes and onions are safer after you create a flat side. In a claw grip, the knuckles guide the knife while the fingertips stay tucked back.

questions.prompt: Before slicing a round potato, what should you do to make it safer?
choices: Balance it on the roundest side
Hold it in the air while cutting
Cut a flat side so it sits steadily
questions.feedback: Creating a flat side helps keep round foods from rolling while you cut them.

questions.prompt: When using a claw grip, what part of your hand should guide the knife?
choices: Your wrist against the cutting edge
questions.feedback: With a claw grip, curled fingers stay back while the knuckles help guide the side of the knife.

miniLessons.title: Keep the Cutting Board Stable
miniLessons.lessonText:
A safe cutting setup keeps both the board and the food under control. The board should not slide, and surfaces that touch raw meat should be easy to clean and sanitize.

For medium questions, think about what makes the station stable and clean. A damp towel can grip under a cutting board. Raw chicken belongs on a clean nonporous cutting board, not on absorbent or hard-to-clean surfaces.

questions.prompt: Why is a damp towel useful under a cutting board?
choices: It helps stop the board from sliding
questions.feedback: A damp towel adds grip between the board and the counter.

questions.prompt: Which surface is safest for cutting raw chicken?
questions.feedback: A nonporous cutting board can be cleaned and sanitized after touching raw chicken.

miniLessons.title: Sharp Knives Are Safer Than Dull Knives
miniLessons.lessonText:
The right knife and safe cleaning habits both reduce risk. A chef’s knife handles many common chopping tasks, but sharp knives should be kept visible and controlled when washing.

For medium questions, match the tool to the task and avoid hidden blades. A chef’s knife is useful for onions, herbs, and vegetables. A sharp knife should be washed separately by hand, not left hidden in soapy water.

questions.prompt: Which knife is generally best for chopping onions, herbs, and vegetables?
questions.feedback: A chef’s knife is versatile and well-suited for chopping many vegetables and herbs.

questions.prompt: What is the safest way to wash a sharp kitchen knife?
choices: Wash it separately by hand with the blade pointed away
Hide it in a sink full of soapy water
Toss it loosely into the dishwasher with the blade up
Hold the blade edge directly in your palm
questions.feedback: Washing a knife separately keeps it visible and helps prevent accidental cuts.

miniLessons.title: Use Heavy Pans for Browning
miniLessons.lessonText:
Different pans handle heat differently. Heavy pans spread and hold heat well, which helps with browning, high-heat cooking, and gentle control for thick sauces.

For medium questions, match the pan to the heat need. A heavy-bottomed saucepan can reduce scorching in custards or thick sauces. A wok or large skillet gives stir-fry ingredients room over high heat.

questions.prompt: Why is a heavy-bottomed saucepan useful for custard or thick sauces?
choices: It spreads heat more evenly and helps reduce scorching
It removes the need to stir
questions.feedback: A heavy-bottomed pan distributes heat more evenly, which helps prevent scorching.

questions.prompt: Which pan is best for making a stir-fry over high heat?
questions.feedback: A wok or large skillet gives food room and can handle the high heat used for stir-frying.

miniLessons.title: Use Nonstick for Delicate Foods
miniLessons.lessonText:
The cooking surface should match the food. Delicate foods need easy release, while acidic foods need cookware that will not react with the acid.

For medium questions, separate sticking problems from reaction problems. Eggs often do best in nonstick. Tomato sauce is acidic, so stainless steel or enameled cookware is safer than reactive uncoated metal.

questions.prompt: You are making an omelet and want it to release easily. Which pan is the best choice?
questions.feedback: A nonstick skillet helps delicate egg dishes release with less sticking.

questions.prompt: Which cookware is most appropriate for simmering an acidic tomato sauce?
choices: A plastic storage bag on the stove
questions.feedback: Stainless steel or enameled cookware is nonreactive and works well for acidic tomato sauce.

miniLessons.title: Use a Sheet Pan for Roasting
miniLessons.lessonText:
Some cooking methods need the right vessel size or material. Boiling needs enough space for liquid and food. Broiling needs cookware that can handle very high direct heat.

For medium questions, look at the cooking method. A large stockpot fits a large batch of pasta with enough water. Broiler-safe metal cookware or cast iron is better under a broiler than plastic, paper, or regular glass.

questions.prompt: Which vessel is best for boiling a large batch of pasta?
questions.feedback: A large stockpot gives pasta enough water and space to cook evenly.

questions.prompt: Which cookware should be used under an oven broiler?
choices: Broiler-safe metal cookware or cast iron
questions.feedback: Broilers use very high direct heat, so the cookware must be broiler-safe.

miniLessons.title: Match the Shape and Job
miniLessons.lessonText:
When replacing a tool, first identify the job: rolling, pressing, brushing, straining, covering, or heating gently. A safe substitute should copy the shape or pressure without adding danger.

For medium questions, think about what the missing tool does physically. A meat mallet flattens with pressure, so a heavy skillet or rolling pin can work carefully. A pastry brush spreads a thin layer, which can also be done with a clean spoon or clean fingers.

questions.prompt: You need to flatten chicken breasts but do not own a meat mallet. What can you use?
choices: A measuring cup full of water with no lid
A heavy skillet or rolling pin
questions.feedback: A heavy skillet or rolling pin can apply even pressure to flatten chicken when used carefully.

questions.prompt: You do not have a pastry brush. What can you use to spread oil lightly over bread or vegetables?
choices: A clean spoon or clean fingers
questions.feedback: A clean spoon or clean fingers can spread a light layer of oil when a pastry brush is not available.

miniLessons.title: Match the Action
miniLessons.lessonText:
Many specialty tools save time, but basic kitchen tools can often perform the same action. The substitute may be slower, but it should still be clean, safe, and effective.

For medium questions, focus on the action. A garlic press crushes garlic finely, but a knife can mince it. A citrus juicer squeezes and releases juice, but rolling the lemon and using a fork can help do the same job.

questions.prompt: You do not have a garlic press. What is the best alternative for finely preparing garlic?
choices: Leave the clove whole and uncooked
Mince it finely with a knife
questions.feedback: A knife can mince garlic finely, even without a garlic press.

questions.prompt: You need to juice a lemon but do not have a citrus juicer. What is the best method?
choices: Put the whole lemon into the dish uncut
Freeze the lemon and never cut it
Roll it, cut it, and squeeze while using a fork to help release juice
questions.feedback: Rolling and squeezing the lemon with a fork helps release more juice without a juicer.

miniLessons.title: Make Safe Setup Swaps
miniLessons.lessonText:
Some swaps involve hot water, rising dough, or other situations where safety matters more than speed. The best workaround should reduce risk while still doing the job.

For medium questions, avoid dangerous shortcuts. Pasta can be lifted with tongs or a slotted spoon if there is no colander. Rising dough can be covered with a clean damp towel or loose lid instead of something dirty, heavy, or unsafe.

questions.prompt: You need to strain pasta but do not have a colander. What is the safest substitute?
choices: Pour boiling water through your fingers
Use a paper towel as a strainer
Dump the pot quickly without holding it securely
Use tongs or a slotted spoon to remove the pasta
questions.feedback: Tongs or a slotted spoon let you remove pasta without pouring a large pot of boiling water.

questions.prompt: You need to cover a bowl of rising dough but do not have plastic wrap. What is the best substitute?
choices: A clean damp towel or loose-fitting lid
A hot skillet directly on the dough
questions.feedback: A clean damp towel or loose lid can protect dough while still giving it room to rise.

miniLessons.title: Read the Recipe Before Replacing Dairy
miniLessons.lessonText: Harder dairy swaps start by reading the recipe around the ingredient. If the recipe uses baking soda, a tangy dairy ingredient may be helping with rise and tenderness, not just adding liquid. If the recipe is creamy or rich, the dairy may also be adding fat and body. A strong swap should replace the main job of the original dairy ingredient instead of only matching the amount.

questions.prompt: A quick bread uses buttermilk and baking soda, but you only have milk, vinegar, butter, and water. Which swap best protects the recipe’s texture?
choices: Milk mixed with a little vinegar, because it adds liquid and acidity
Water, because it has the same volume as buttermilk
Melted butter, because it is dairy fat
Plain milk only, because all dairy works the same way
questions.feedback: Buttermilk adds moisture and acidity. Milk with a little vinegar is a closer functional match, especially when baking soda is involved.

questions.prompt: A potato soup recipe calls for whole milk near the end. You only have evaporated milk. Which choice is most likely to keep the soup creamy without making it too concentrated?
choices: Use evaporated milk powder-dry without liquid
Dilute evaporated milk with water before using it as a milk substitute
Use only butter and no liquid
questions.feedback: Evaporated milk is concentrated. Diluting it helps it behave more like regular milk while still adding creaminess.

miniLessons.title: Match Thickness, Tang, and Use
miniLessons.lessonText: Some dairy substitutes only need to taste similar, while others must also behave similarly. A topping needs thickness so it sits on the food. A filling needs body so it holds its shape. A tangy ingredient needs another mild acidic flavor if the tang matters. The best choice depends on how the dairy is being used in that specific dish.

questions.prompt: A lasagna filling calls for ricotta, but you only have cottage cheese, milk, butter, and plain yogurt. Which option is the best starting point?
choices: Use milk because it is dairy
Use butter because it is rich
Drain or blend cottage cheese so it better matches ricotta’s soft texture
Use plain yogurt because all thick dairy works in lasagna
questions.feedback: Cottage cheese is soft and mild like ricotta. Draining or blending it improves the texture for a filling.

questions.prompt: A dip needs cream cheese for thickness and spreadability. Which substitute is most likely to keep the dip close to the original texture?
questions.feedback: Neufchâtel is close to cream cheese in texture and use, while thin liquids would make the dip loose.

miniLessons.title: Expect Tradeoffs in Rich Dairy Swaps
miniLessons.lessonText: Rich dairy ingredients can be difficult to replace because they add more than one thing at once. Cream, half-and-half, and whole milk all bring different amounts of fat, liquid, and body. When the recipe does not need whipping, you usually have more flexibility. The goal is to keep the finished dish from becoming thin, greasy, bland, or too heavy.

questions.prompt: A pasta sauce calls for half-and-half, but you only have milk and heavy cream. Which option best recreates half-and-half?
choices: Use a mixture of milk and heavy cream
Use dry flour instead of dairy
questions.feedback: Half-and-half is between milk and cream in richness, so combining milk and cream creates a closer substitute.

questions.prompt: A soup calls for heavy cream, but the cream is not being whipped. Which option best explains why milk plus butter can work better than milk alone?
choices: The butter makes the soup acidic
The butter helps replace some of the fat that heavy cream would have added
The butter makes the soup boil faster
questions.feedback: Heavy cream adds both liquid and fat. Milk supplies liquid, while butter helps replace some richness.

miniLessons.title: Adjust for Concentration
miniLessons.lessonText: Harder seasoning substitutions often come down to concentration. Dried herbs, ground spices, and powders can be stronger than their fresh versions because the flavor is more concentrated. That means a substitute may need a smaller amount even when it sounds like the same ingredient. Starting with less also gives you room to taste and adjust.

questions.prompt: A sauce calls for 2 tablespoons fresh oregano, but you only have dried oregano. What is the best first amount to try?
choices: No oregano because dried herbs cannot substitute for fresh herbs
questions.feedback: Dried herbs are often used at about one-third the fresh amount. Since 2 tablespoons equals 6 teaspoons, about 2 teaspoons is a reasonable starting point.

questions.prompt: A recipe calls for two fresh garlic cloves, but you only have garlic powder. Which approach is safest for flavor balance?
choices: Use the same volume of garlic powder as chopped garlic
Use a full tablespoon for each clove
Skip measuring because garlic powder is weak
Use a small measured amount of garlic powder because it is concentrated
questions.feedback: Garlic powder is concentrated. A small measured amount is safer than matching the volume of fresh garlic.

miniLessons.title: Substitute by Flavor Family
miniLessons.lessonText: When the exact herb or spice is missing, think about the flavor family. Some seasonings are earthy, some are bright and leafy, some are warm and sweet, and others are smoky or hot. A good substitute usually belongs to a similar family and fits the type of dish. This is more reliable than choosing an ingredient just because it is also an herb or spice.

questions.prompt: A roasted chicken recipe calls for rosemary, but you are out. Which substitute fits the savory, herbal direction best?
questions.feedback: Thyme is savory and herbal, so it fits roasted chicken better than sweet baking spices.

questions.prompt: A recipe uses cumin for an earthy, warm flavor. Which substitute is most reasonable if cumin is missing?
choices: Ground coriander with a small amount of chili powder
questions.feedback: Coriander and chili powder can move in a similar warm, savory direction, though the flavor will not be identical.

miniLessons.title: Rebuild Blends From Their Purpose
miniLessons.lessonText: Seasoning blends are shortcuts. If a blend is missing, the best substitute depends on what kind of blend it is. Warm baking blends often use spices like cinnamon, ginger, nutmeg, and cloves. Savory herb blends often use basil, oregano, thyme, rosemary, or parsley. The substitute does not need to be perfect, but it should point the dish in the same direction.

questions.prompt: You are out of pumpkin pie spice but have several individual spices. Which combination best matches its usual flavor role?
choices: Garlic powder, onion powder, and paprika
questions.feedback: Pumpkin pie spice is a warm baking blend. Cinnamon, ginger, nutmeg, and cloves create a similar profile.

questions.prompt: You are out of Italian seasoning for a tomato pasta sauce. Which blend is the best replacement?
choices: Dried basil, oregano, thyme, and rosemary
questions.feedback: Italian seasoning is a savory herb blend, so dried basil, oregano, thyme, and rosemary are a better match.

miniLessons.title: Choose a Binder for the Structure Needed
miniLessons.lessonText: Harder binder questions ask what the ingredient is holding together. Eggs, flax mixtures, mashed fruit, breadcrumbs, oats, and starches can all help with structure in different situations. A good substitute should match the kind of food being made. Sweet baked goods can handle fruit-based binders more often than savory loaves, while savory foods often need neutral binders.

questions.prompt: A vegan muffin recipe needs one egg mostly for binding, not for rise. Which substitute best matches that job?
questions.feedback: A flaxseed-and-water mixture forms a gel that can help bind muffins when the egg is mainly for structure.

questions.prompt: A savory lentil loaf is falling apart and the recipe calls for bread crumbs as a binder. You are out. Which substitute is most useful?
questions.feedback: Rolled oats or crushed crackers can absorb moisture and help hold a savory loaf together.

miniLessons.title: Match the Thickener to the Food
miniLessons.lessonText: Thickeners do not all behave exactly the same way. Cornstarch, flour, arrowroot, and tapioca starch can thicken liquids, but they may change the look, strength, or texture of the final dish. Fruit fillings and glossy sauces often work well with starches like arrowroot. Creamy sauces may tolerate flour, but usually need more flour than cornstarch.

questions.prompt: A fruit filling needs cornstarch, but you are out. Which substitute is most likely to thicken clearly without changing the flavor much?
questions.feedback: Arrowroot starch can thicken fruit fillings and is often a close substitute for cornstarch.

questions.prompt: A sauce calls for 1 tablespoon of cornstarch, but you only have flour. Which swap is most reasonable?
choices: Use no thickener because flour cannot thicken
questions.feedback: Flour has less thickening power than cornstarch, so using about twice as much flour is a common adjustment.

miniLessons.title: Use the Correct Technique for Texture
miniLessons.lessonText: The right thickener can still fail if it is added poorly. Dry starch added straight to hot liquid can form lumps. A slurry spreads the starch through cold liquid first, making it easier to mix smoothly into hot food. If lumps already formed, whisking or straining may rescue the texture before serving.

questions.prompt: You need to thicken hot gravy with starch. Which method gives you the best chance of avoiding lumps?
choices: Mix the starch with cold liquid first, then whisk it into the hot gravy
Sprinkle dry starch into the hottest spot without stirring
Add the starch after the gravy is already frozen
Mix starch with oil only and never stir
questions.feedback: A cold slurry helps the starch disperse before it reaches the hot liquid, which lowers the chance of lumps.

questions.prompt: A pudding has small starch lumps after cooking. Which fix is most likely to improve it before chilling?
choices: Add more dry starch directly to the hot pudding
Whisk well and strain the pudding through a fine mesh strainer
Stir in raw eggs without tempering
Freeze it with the lumps still inside
questions.feedback: Whisking may smooth some lumps, and straining can remove pieces that do not dissolve.

miniLessons.title: Find the Scaling Factor First
miniLessons.lessonText: Hard scaling problems are easier when you find the scaling factor before touching the ingredients. Compare the new yield to the old yield. If the new recipe is bigger, multiply. If it is smaller, divide. Once the factor is clear, use it on the ingredients that normally scale directly, like broth, flour, pasta, rice, or cheese.

questions.prompt: A chili recipe serves 6 and uses 3 cans of beans. You need to serve 10 people. What is the best way to scale the beans?
choices: Use 3 cans because beans do not scale
Use 6 cans because 10 is close to double 6
Use 1 can because the serving size changed
questions.feedback: The scaling factor is 10/6, or 5/3. Three cans times 5/3 equals five cans.

questions.prompt: A recipe makes 18 meatballs with 1 1/2 cups of breadcrumbs. How much is needed for 30 meatballs?
questions.feedback: Thirty meatballs is 30/18, or 5/3, of the original. 1 1/2 cups times 5/3 equals 2 1/2 cups.

miniLessons.title: Handle Fractions Without Changing the Ratio
miniLessons.lessonText: Fractions show up often in recipe scaling. The safest method is to scale the recipe size first, then apply the same multiplier to the ingredient amount. If the recipe is one-third as large, use one-third of the ingredient. If it is one-and-a-half times as large, multiply the ingredient by 1.5. The goal is not to make the numbers pretty; the goal is to keep the recipe balanced.

questions.prompt: A sauce recipe makes 4 cups and uses 2/3 cup cream. You only need 3 cups of sauce. How much cream should you use?
questions.feedback: Three cups is 3/4 of the original recipe. 2/3 cup times 3/4 equals 1/2 cup.

questions.prompt: A recipe makes 8 servings and uses 3/4 teaspoon pepper. You want 12 servings. How much pepper keeps the same ratio?
questions.feedback: Twelve servings is 1.5 times the original. 3/4 teaspoon times 1.5 equals 1 1/8 teaspoons.

miniLessons.title: Know What Does Not Scale Perfectly
miniLessons.lessonText: Most base ingredients scale mathematically, but cooking time, pan size, heat, and seasoning may need judgment. Doubling a soup does not always mean doubling the cooking time. Increasing salt or hot spices may be safer in stages because flavor can become too strong quickly. Hard scaling means using the math first, then checking whether the cooking method still makes sense.

questions.prompt: You double a soup recipe. Which adjustment is safest for salt?
choices: Never add salt to a doubled recipe
Add less than the full doubled amount at first, taste, then adjust
Add all the doubled salt before tasting
questions.feedback: Salt can become overpowering. Adding it in stages gives you control while still using the scaling math as a guide.

questions.prompt: You double a sheet-pan vegetable recipe but use the same pan. What is the most likely issue?
choices: The vegetables will automatically cook twice as fast
The vegetables will not need oil
The vegetables may crowd and steam instead of brown
questions.feedback: More food in the same pan can trap moisture and reduce browning. You may need another pan or a larger surface.

miniLessons.title: Diagnose Before You Add More
miniLessons.lessonText: Hard flavor fixes start with diagnosis. Bland food may need salt, but food can also taste flat because it lacks acidity, freshness, aroma, or contrast. If the dish is already salty enough, more salt is not the answer. Try to identify what is missing, then make a small adjustment and taste again before adding more.

questions.prompt: A finished bean soup tastes flat, but it is already salty enough. Which adjustment is most likely to help?
choices: Add a small splash of vinegar or lemon juice
Boil until all liquid is gone
questions.feedback: If salt is already sufficient, acid can add brightness and make the flavor feel more complete.

questions.prompt: A rice bowl tastes heavy and one-dimensional, but not bland. Which addition is most likely to add contrast without increasing saltiness?
choices: Fresh herbs and a squeeze of citrus
A large amount of baking soda
questions.feedback: Fresh herbs and citrus add aroma and brightness without relying on more salt.

miniLessons.title: Balance One Strong Flavor With Its Opposite
miniLessons.lessonText: When one flavor is too strong, the goal is usually balance, not hiding the problem completely. Too acidic can sometimes be softened with a tiny amount of sweetness or fat. Too sweet can be balanced with acid, bitterness, salt, or dilution depending on the dish. Too rich can often be lifted with acidity or freshness. Make small changes because overcorrecting creates a new problem.

questions.prompt: A tomato sauce tastes sharp and acidic. Which first adjustment is most reasonable?
choices: Add a full cup of sugar
Add a small pinch of sugar and taste again
Add lemon juice until it tastes brighter
questions.feedback: A small pinch of sugar can soften acidity. A large amount would make the sauce too sweet.

questions.prompt: A glaze tastes too sweet. Which adjustment is most likely to rebalance it?
choices: Remove all liquid and keep cooking until burnt
Add a small amount of acid, such as vinegar or lemon juice
questions.feedback: Acid can cut sweetness and make the flavor more balanced when used carefully.

miniLessons.title: Rescue Heat, Salt, and Bitterness Carefully
miniLessons.lessonText: Emergency fixes work best when they address the actual problem. Spicy heat can feel milder when creamy, fatty, starchy, or mild ingredients are added. Saltiness is usually reduced by dilution or adding unsalted ingredients. Bitterness from burned garlic or spices may not disappear, so the safest fix may be removing the burned pieces or starting that part over.

questions.prompt: A curry is too spicy, but the flavor is otherwise good. Which fix keeps the dish closest to the original?
choices: Add coconut milk and extra mild ingredients gradually
Boil it until the heat disappears
Add dry flour directly to the curry
questions.feedback: Creamy and mild ingredients can reduce the perceived heat while keeping the curry’s flavor direction.

questions.prompt: Garlic burned in oil and tastes bitter before the rest of the sauce is added. What is the best immediate response?
choices: Add sugar until the bitterness disappears
Discard the burned garlic and oil, then restart that step
Add more raw garlic to cover it
Keep cooking because bitterness always fades
questions.feedback: Burned garlic can make a dish bitter. It is often better to restart that step than build a sauce on a bitter base.

miniLessons.title: Choose the Right Texture Direction
miniLessons.lessonText: Texture fixes begin by naming the problem: too thin, too thick, lumpy, watery, dry, separated, or not crisp. Each problem needs a different response. Thickening requires starch, reduction, or an absorbing ingredient. Thinning requires liquid added slowly. Lumps need whisking or straining. The best fix depends on both the problem and the food.

questions.prompt: A cream soup is thin, but the flavor is already strong. Which fix thickens it without adding much extra flavor?
choices: Whisk in a cornstarch slurry and simmer briefly
Add uncooked pasta and serve immediately
questions.feedback: A slurry can thicken the soup without dramatically changing the flavor when used carefully.

questions.prompt: A dip is too thick after chilling. Which fix gives the most control?
choices: Pour in a full cup of liquid immediately
Stir in a small amount of milk or water at a time
